title: Te Aho Pāngarau o Te Awa: The Mathematical Threads of Our River / Year 10 Mathematics: Statistical Analysis of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Mathematics
culturalContext: Students will investigate the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our college grounds and holds deep significance to Ngāti Kahungunu. According to local pūrākau, the stream was named for the kotukutuku (tree fuchsia) that once lined its banks, providing medicinal resources and habitat for native birds. Students will collect and analyze water quality data while considering the mathematical patterns that reflect the mauri (life force) of our awa.
learningObjectives: Collect and organize water quality data (pH, turbidity, temperature) from three locations along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using appropriate measurement tools
Calculate and interpret measures of central tendency (mean, median, mode) and spread (range) for each data set to identify patterns and variations
Create and analyze comparative statistical graphs (box-and-whisker plots, bar graphs) to visually represent the health indicators of the awa
Draw evidence-based conclusions about the water quality and propose mathematically justified recommendations for stream restoration

activities.title: Whakatau: Honoring Our Awa
activities.description: Begin with a karakia to acknowledge Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Local kaumātua will share the pūrākau of the stream and discuss its significance to Ngāti Kahungunu. Students will learn proper protocols for engaging with the awa and understand why we measure its health mathematically.

activities.title: Pūkenga Inenga: Measurement Skills Development
activities.description: Hands-on workshop using water testing kits (pH strips, turbidity tubes, thermometers) to practice accurate measurement techniques. Students will learn to record data systematically and create frequency tables for organization, ensuring consistency across measurement teams.

activities.title: Tikanga Raraunga: Data Collection Mahi
activities.description: Field trip to three strategic locations along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (upstream near the source, mid-stream by college grounds, downstream near confluence). Working in small groups, students will collect multiple measurements at each site following scientific protocols.

activities.title: Tātari Tau: Statistical Analysis
activities.description: Back in classroom, students will calculate mean, median, mode, and range for each data set. Using digital tools (Google Sheets/Excel), they will create comparative box-and-whisker plots and bar graphs to visualize the variations between sampling locations.

activities.title: Whakamahere Awa: Data-Informed Action Planning
activities.description: Students will interpret their statistical findings to assess the health of the awa. They will use their mathematical evidence to propose specific, measurable restoration actions (e.g., 'Planting 50 native trees along the bank where turbidity readings exceeded X value') and present findings to local environmental group.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua knowledgeable about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku pūrākau and tikanga
Hawke's Bay Regional Council water quality testing kits and monitoring protocols
NIWA's 'River and Stream Monitoring' educational resources and data comparison tools
Mangakōtukutuku College's historical water quality data archive (2015-present)

tasks: Complete data collection table with accurate measurements from three stream locations
Calculate and present measures of central tendency and spread for each data set
Create two comparative statistical graphs that effectively visualize the water quality data
Write a conclusion paragraph that uses mathematical evidence to assess the awa's health and propose one specific restoration action

nzcAlignment: Statistics: Statistical investigation - Plan and conduct surveys, experiments, and observational studies
Statistics: Statistical literacy - Evaluate statistical reports and trends in context
Number: Calculation and estimation - Calculate and estimate with decimals and percentages
Key Competencies: Using language, symbols and texts - Interpret statistical information and graphs
